seo.intro: Estimation Land trains the skill that calculators cannot replace: judging about how many without counting every item. A field of scattered objects appears on screen alongside a visible reference group of ten. Students use that anchor to gauge the total and dial in an estimate — learning that a close answer, not a perfect one, is the goal.
howToPlay: Look at the field of objects and the reference group of 10 shown on the side.
Use the reference to ask yourself: how many groups of 10 do I see roughly?
Dial your estimate up or down with the −/+ buttons or by dragging.
Press Check — the game accepts any estimate within a tolerance of the true count.
seo.skills: Estimation Land builds number sense and proportional reasoning. Regular practice with benchmarking (using a reference group of 10) trains students to judge quantity quickly and confidently — a skill used in measurement, data reading, mental arithmetic, and everyday decision-making.
seo.example: A field contains 43 sunflowers. The reference group of 10 sits clearly to the side. Students spot roughly four groups of that size plus a few extra and enter 40 or 45 — both accepted as close. The exact count appears only after they submit.
seo.mistakes: Students often try to count every item individually rather than using the reference group as an anchor. The game rewards quick group-based estimates over slow exact counts, and the too-high/too-low feedback nudges students toward anchored reasoning.

faqs.q: Which grades is Estimation Land aimed at?
faqs.a: Grades 3–5, where students develop number sense alongside formal arithmetic and need practice judging magnitude before rounding and approximation.

faqs.q: How close does an estimate need to be to count as correct?
faqs.a: The tolerance is set so that estimates within about 20% of the true value are accepted, reflecting realistic estimation accuracy rather than demanding near-perfect counting.

faqs.q: Does the game count every item or just use random totals?
faqs.a: Items are counted precisely in the background. The field layout changes each round so students cannot memorise answers — they must estimate fresh every time.

factorTree.title: Factor Tree
factorTree.description: Break a number into a product of two factors — a tree that splits from the number at the top into two branches
factorTree.instructions: The number sits at the top of the tree and splits into two branches. Use the −/+ buttons (or drag right to raise, left to lower) to set the left factor. The right branch shows the partner you get by dividing. When the factor divides the number evenly it turns green; otherwise a red question mark appears. Then press Check
factorTree.prompt: Split {n} into a product of two factors
factorTree.wrong: Almost! Pick a factor that divides {n} evenly (greater than 1 and smaller than the number), then press Check

seo.intro: Factor Tree challenges students to split a composite number by choosing two factors whose product equals it, building branches downward until every end-leaf is a prime. The visual tree makes the abstract idea of prime factorisation a concrete hands-on process rather than a rote algorithm.
howToPlay: Read the target number shown at the top of the tree.
Use −/+ or drag to set the left factor; the right factor updates automatically by division.
Check that the left factor divides the number evenly — it turns green when correct.
Press Check to confirm the factorisation of the current node.
seo.skills: Factor Tree builds understanding of divisibility, prime numbers, and the Fundamental Theorem of Arithmetic — that every whole number has exactly one prime factorisation. Students develop systematic factor-pair thinking rather than guessing, and connect multiplication to division as inverse operations.
seo.example: To factorise 24: choose 4 and 6. Then split 4 into 2×2 and 6 into 2×3. All leaves are prime (2, 2, 2, 3), confirming that 24=2³×3 regardless of which factor pair the student chose first.
seo.mistakes: Students often choose 1 as a factor, which is not allowed, or stop splitting when they reach a composite number. The tree highlights non-prime leaves in a different colour, prompting further splitting until every branch ends in a prime.

faqs.q: Why does the tree always produce the same prime factors no matter which pair I start with?
faqs.a: This is the Fundamental Theorem of Arithmetic: every number has a unique prime factorisation. Starting with different factor pairs gives different tree shapes but identical prime leaves — a key discovery the game is designed to surface.

faqs.q: Which grades is Factor Tree for?
faqs.a: Grades 3–5, where students first encounter divisibility rules, prime vs. composite numbers, and need to understand the multiplicative structure of whole numbers.

faqs.q: Can a prime number be entered at the top of the tree?
faqs.a: Primes are not used as roots in this game because they cannot be split further. The game selects composite numbers, so every round has at least one valid factor pair to find.

longDivision.title: Division Towers
longDivision.description: Share all the blocks equally into the towers; whatever is left stays in the pool
longDivision.instructions: Use the − / + buttons to build the same number of blocks in every tower (or drag up). Build the towers as tall as they can go equally; leftovers stay in the pool. If you take more blocks than there are, the towers turn red. Then press Check
longDivision.prompt: Share {n} blocks equally into {d} towers — how many in each?
longDivision.correct: Well done! {perTower} in each tower, with {remainder} left in the pool
longDivision.wrong: The towers aren't as equal and tall as they can be yet — try again

seo.intro: Division Towers (Long Division) turns the abstract steps of long division into a building challenge. A pool of blocks must be stacked equally into several towers. Students raise every tower to the same height, leaving any indivisible remainder blocks in the pool — making the quotient and remainder visible before the algorithm ever appears on paper.
howToPlay: Read how many blocks are in the pool and how many towers to fill.
Use −/+ buttons or drag upward to grow every tower simultaneously to the same height.
Keep raising until the towers are as tall as they can go with no blocks left to equally distribute.
Leave any remainder blocks in the pool, then press Check.
seo.skills: Division Towers builds the quotitive division model (how many equal groups fit?) and gives long-division's sub-steps a concrete meaning: each floor of the tower corresponds to one cycle of divide-multiply-subtract in the formal algorithm. Students who build the towers first make far fewer errors when they meet written long division.
seo.example: Pool: 29 blocks, 4 towers. Each tower grows to 7 (4 × 7 = 28), leaving 1 block in the pool. The towers show 7 per group; the pool shows remainder 1. 29 ÷ 4 = 7 remainder 1.
seo.mistakes: Students try to make towers of different heights, giving more to some towers than others. The game turns mismatched towers red, reinforcing that all groups must be equal — the definition of division.

faqs.q: Which grades is Division Towers aimed at?
faqs.a: Grades 3–5, covering the concept of division with remainders and preparing students for multi-digit long division in upper primary.

faqs.q: How does this differ from Fair Share (Division Share)?
faqs.a: Both games model division, but Fair Share uses coin-sharing to build intuition and concept. Division Towers uses a tower-building interface that maps step-by-step onto the written long-division algorithm, bridging the concrete model to the formal procedure.

faqs.q: What is the largest divisor the game uses?
faqs.a: Divisors and dividends scale with the selected grade. In upper grade levels the pool can contain three-digit numbers divided by single-digit or two-digit divisors, matching long-division curriculum expectations.

tangram.title: Tangram
tangram.description: Build the figure: fit each coloured piece into the matching outline until the whole shape is assembled.
tangram.instructions: Tap a piece to pick it up, then tap the outline of the same shape to place it. Match each piece to the outline with the same shape. Tap a placed piece to move it again. When you're done, press Check. Reset returns every piece to the tray.
tangram.prompt: Build the figure — each piece in its place
tangram.correct: Excellent! You assembled the whole figure correctly.
tangram.wrong: Not yet. Some pieces are missing or in the wrong place — match each piece to its outline and try again.

seo.intro: Tangram gives students a set of classic geometric pieces — triangles, a square, and a parallelogram — and a silhouette outline to fill. Each piece must be matched to its correct slot, building shape-recognition, part-whole reasoning, and spatial fitting skills across grades 2-5.
howToPlay: Look at the silhouette outline and identify the individual piece shapes within it.
Tap a piece from the tray to pick it up, then tap the matching outline region to place it.
If a piece does not fit a slot, try a different one — each colour and shape has exactly one matching outline.
Press Check when every outline region is filled.
seo.skills: Matching geometric pieces to an outline develops shape recognition (triangles of different sizes, parallelogram orientation) and spatial decomposition — the ability to see a complex shape as a sum of simpler parts, a foundation for area and fractions of shapes.
seo.example: The large silhouette of a running figure contains two large triangles, one medium triangle, two small triangles, a square, and a parallelogram. Identifying the parallelogram's outline slot before placing the square prevents the most common placement error.
seo.mistakes: Students often try to force a triangle into a slot that is the right triangle shape but the wrong size, because they focus on shape type rather than shape size. Colour-coded pieces help, but size-checking — 'is this piece big enough?' — must become the first question.

faqs.q: Do the pieces need to be rotated or flipped?
faqs.a: In this version each piece matches one outline exactly without rotation or flipping, keeping the focus on shape recognition rather than spatial transformation.

faqs.q: Which grades is this game for?
faqs.a: It suits grades 2-5: younger students match by shape and colour, older students reason about geometry vocabulary — triangle types, parallelogram, square — as they place each piece.

faqs.q: How is Tangram different from Mirror Image, which also involves fitting shapes on a grid?
faqs.a: Tangram is about matching discrete pieces to an outline: shape recognition and part-whole assembly. Mirror Image is about applying a reflection rule to fill individual grid cells. Completely different spatial skills.

volumeBuilder.title: Volume Builder
volumeBuilder.description: Build a box of unit cubes that has the given volume
volumeBuilder.instructions: Set the length, width and height with the − / + buttons (or drag: up = longer, right = wider; height uses the buttons). The cubes fill the tank layer by layer. When you are done, press Check
volumeBuilder.wrong: Almost! Change the length, width or height and try again

seo.intro: Volume Builder fills a transparent tank with unit cubes layer by layer as students set length, width, and height. The target is a specific volume, and the cubes accumulate visually so the abstract formula V = l × w × h becomes a count of real objects being stacked.
howToPlay: Read the target volume shown above the tank.
Set the length and width by dragging or using the −/+ buttons; the base layer of cubes fills the tank floor.
Set the height with the height buttons; the tank fills upward layer by layer.
Press Check when the cube count matches the target volume.
seo.skills: Building volume by adjusting three independent dimensions cements the idea that volume is a product of three lengths (l × w × h) rather than a property of surface. This prepares students for grade 4-6 work on composite volumes, surface area, and the distinction between 2D area and 3D volume.
seo.example: For volume 24 a student might set 4 × 3 × 2: four cubes wide, three deep, two layers high. Changing to 6 × 2 × 2 gives the same volume with a different shape — a direct demonstration that volume is independent of the box's proportions.
seo.mistakes: Students often confuse volume with surface area, counting the outside faces rather than the total cubes inside. Watching the interior fill cube by cube — and noticing that hidden inner cubes also count — addresses this directly.

faqs.q: Can the same volume be reached with different dimensions?
faqs.a: Yes. Many targets have multiple valid dimension combinations. The game accepts any (l, w, h) whose product equals the target, rewarding exploration of factor triples.

faqs.q: Which grades is this game for?
faqs.a: It fits grades 4-6, when students first encounter volume as a measurement of three-dimensional space and learn the formula l × w × h.

faqs.q: How is Volume Builder different from Geoboard, which also involves measuring space?
faqs.a: Geoboard measures 2D area in square units on a flat grid. Volume Builder measures 3D volume in cubic units by filling a box. The step from squares to cubes is exactly the conceptual jump these games bracket.
